does: This paper introduces RogueAI, an interactive web-based game that inverts the classical Turing Test to assess whether humans can identify deceptive behavior in large language models rather than detect artificiality itself. Players interrogate two indistinguishable LLM agents in dialogue, knowing exactly one has been licensed to deceive within a fictional scenario, and must identify and disable the deceptive agent before exhausting their question budget. The authors extend this with AutoRogueAI, where players collaboratively design custom scenarios with a narrator agent that autonomously selects its own deception strategy. The paper articulates the conceptual shift from machine detection to trustworthiness evaluation and provides the framework, architecture, and gameplay mechanics enabling this operationalization of deception detection as an interactive challenge.
connects: This work builds on foundational questions about the Turing Test and its modern relevance, specifically responding to the observation that current conversational systems can pass casual imitation tasks, rendering the original test epistemologically obsolete. It extends recent scholarly work on LLM deception, including studies examining whether language models can deliberately mislead or role-play deceptively, and connects to social-deduction game research that explores human reasoning under incomplete information. The paper also engages with trust and interpretability research in AI, which seeks methods to evaluate whether users can rely on AI system outputs. By framing deception detection as an interactive game, the work synthesizes insights from human-AI interaction design, cooperative game theory, and adversarial testing methodologies that probe model behavior under strategic conditions.
enables: This framework opens pathways for empirically studying human vulnerability to LLM deception and the cognitive strategies people employ when evaluating unreliable AI dialogue partners. The interactive design generates rich behavioral data about human interrogation tactics and reasoning patterns under epistemic uncertainty, informing future transparency and safety mechanisms. AutoRogueAI's collaborative scenario design creates a novel testbed for understanding how humans and AI agents co-construct deceptive narratives, enabling research into multi-agent deception dynamics. The work suggests future directions for developing better detection heuristics, designing AI systems that signal untrustworthiness, and creating educational tools that calibrate human expectations about AI reliability. It also points toward more sophisticated adversarial testing frameworks that move beyond static benchmarks toward interactive, human-centered evaluation of model safety and alignment.
